evidence_quotes.quote: Videogames as of late have been a prominent target for some videogame publishers making use of online-only servers to temporarily support the game in question, and when it no longer is seen as profitable, to shut it down permanently, as has happened with "the crew" and many others.

paragraphs.text: Hello, My name is Juan Cachafeiro, I am a citizen of Spain and I'm glad to see legal regulation aimed at protecting the consumer in a setting so predatory as the digital medium can be, however, if I may, I wanted to bring to your attention an issue adjacent to the one being addressed, videogames. Videogames as of late have been a prominent target for some videogame publishers making use of online-only servers to temporarily support the game in question, and when it no longer is seen as profitable, to shut it down permanently, as has happened with "the crew" and many others. These games are products we have already bought and what is advertised as the product is not just the temporary license to use it, but the game itself, so by selling these games without any kind of plan to maintain support or at least a warning that the game will no longer be able to played after a certain amount of time, the publishers are effectively destroying a product we already own, for this I wanted to ask if it would be possible, to integrate games and the protection of their integrity as a product for consumers that have paid for them, to the legislature present on this act. As you might've seen recently, the "stop killing games initiative" has garnered an immense amount of support within the EU, making the concern around this topic quite difficult to ignore, and since this proposal addresses themes adjacent to videogames, as digital products, I wondered if there was any way to maybe discuss the indexing of videogames, as digital media, to the elements protected within this act. I would greatly appreciate your kindly consideration of this topic within the bounds of possible actions to take with this proposal.
